module.title: SUBQUERIES
module.subtitle: scalar · IN (subquery) · correlated · derived table
module.theory:
## What a subquery is
A **subquery** is a SELECT tucked inside another query. The inner query runs first, hands its result to the outer query, and the outer query uses it. Think of it as asking a small question to answer a bigger one.

## 1. Scalar subquery (returns ONE value)
A scalar subquery returns a single value, so you can compare a column against it in **WHERE**.
~~~sql
SELECT handle, payout FROM runners
WHERE payout > (SELECT AVG(payout) FROM runners);
~~~
**WHAT** it does: the inner query computes the average payout once; the outer query keeps only rows above it. **WHY** use it: you cannot put AVG() directly in a WHERE clause, so you wrap it in a subquery.

> INTEL — A scalar subquery must return exactly one row and one column. Use AVG/MAX/MIN/COUNT, or a single-row lookup like (SELECT rep FROM runners WHERE handle = 'Cipher').

## 2. WHERE col IN (SELECT ...)
When the inner query returns a *list* of values, test membership with **IN**.
~~~sql
SELECT handle FROM runners
WHERE id IN (SELECT runner_id FROM contracts);
~~~
**WHAT** it does: the inner query lists every runner_id that holds a contract; the outer query keeps runners whose id is in that list. **WHY**: it answers "which rows match something in another table?" without writing a JOIN. Flip it to **NOT IN** for the opposite group.

> WARNING — NOT IN behaves strangely if the inner list contains NULL. Keep the inner column NULL-free (as ours is) and you are safe.

## 3. Correlated subquery (runs once per outer row)
A **correlated** subquery refers back to the outer query, so it re-runs for each outer row.
~~~sql
SELECT handle, sector FROM runners r
WHERE payout = (SELECT MAX(payout) FROM runners s WHERE s.sector = r.sector);
~~~
**WHAT** it does: for each runner r, the inner query finds the top payout *in that runner's own sector*; we keep r only if it owns that top payout. **WHY**: it compares each row to its own group. The table aliases **r** and **s** are what link the inner query to the outer one.

## 4. Derived table — FROM (SELECT ...) alias
You can SELECT from the result of another SELECT. That inner result is a **derived table**, and it MUST be given an alias.
~~~sql
SELECT sector, avg_rep
FROM (SELECT sector, AVG(rep) AS avg_rep FROM runners GROUP BY sector) t
WHERE avg_rep >= 70;
~~~
**WHAT** it does: the inner query builds a mini-table of one row per sector with its average rep; the outer query filters that mini-table. **WHY**: it lets you filter or sort on an aggregate you just computed.

> WARNING — Forgetting the alias after the closing parenthesis (the **t** above) is the #1 beginner mistake. SQLite rejects a derived table with no name.

## The grid
~~~text
runners(id, handle, sector, rep, payout)
contracts(id, runner_id, target, bounty)
~~~

exercises.title: OVERPAID
exercises.prompt: The **runners(id, handle, sector, rep, payout)** table lists every netrunner on the grid. Return the **handle** and **payout** of each runner whose **payout** is **strictly greater than the average payout** of all runners. Two columns. Any order.
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.solution: SELECT handle, payout FROM runners WHERE payout > (SELECT AVG(payout) FROM runners);
exercises.hint: Wrap the average in a subquery: WHERE payout > (SELECT AVG(payout) FROM runners).
exercises.lore: The fixer pays by the megabyte. Most of us never see the mean.

exercises.title: OUTCLASS CIPHER
exercises.prompt: Return the **handle** of every runner whose **rep** is **strictly higher than Cipher's rep**. Look up Cipher's rep with a subquery (do not hard-code a number). One column, sorted by handle (A to Z).
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.starter: SELECT handle FROM runners ORDER BY handle;
exercises.solution: SELECT handle FROM runners WHERE rep > (SELECT rep FROM runners WHERE handle = 'Cipher') ORDER BY handle;
exercises.hint: Compare rep to (SELECT rep FROM runners WHERE handle = 'Cipher').
exercises.lore: Cipher set the bar. A few ghosts cleared it.

exercises.title: ON THE BOARD
exercises.prompt: The **contracts(id, runner_id, target, bounty)** table records who is on a job. Return the **handle** of every runner that holds **at least one contract** — match runners.id against the runner_id values inside contracts using **IN (SELECT ...)**. One column, sorted by handle (A to Z).
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.starter: SELECT handle FROM runners ORDER BY handle;
exercises.solution: SELECT handle FROM runners WHERE id IN (SELECT runner_id FROM contracts) ORDER BY handle;
exercises.hint: WHERE id IN (SELECT runner_id FROM contracts).
exercises.lore: If your tag is on the board, you are already burning daylight.

exercises.title: BENCHED
exercises.prompt: Return the **handle** of every runner that holds **no contract at all** — use **NOT IN (SELECT runner_id FROM contracts)**. One column, sorted by handle (A to Z).
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.starter: SELECT handle FROM runners ORDER BY handle;
exercises.solution: SELECT handle FROM runners WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT runner_id FROM contracts) ORDER BY handle;
exercises.hint: Flip the membership test: WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT runner_id FROM contracts).
exercises.lore: No job, no payout. Just neon and cold noodles.

exercises.title: RICH SEAM
exercises.brief: Everyone in the top earner's sector.
exercises.prompt: Find the sector of the single highest-paid runner, then return the **handle** of **every** runner working in that **same sector**. Use a scalar subquery to get the top earner's sector. One column, sorted by handle (A to Z).
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.starter: SELECT handle FROM runners ORDER BY handle;
exercises.solution: SELECT handle FROM runners WHERE sector = (SELECT sector FROM runners ORDER BY payout DESC LIMIT 1) ORDER BY handle;
exercises.hint: Inner query: (SELECT sector FROM runners ORDER BY payout DESC LIMIT 1). Compare sector to it.
exercises.lore: Money clusters. Find the seam, work the seam.

exercises.title: LOCAL KINGS
exercises.prompt: Return the **handle** and **sector** of the runner with the **highest payout in their own sector** (one per sector). Use a **correlated subquery** that compares each runner's payout to the MAX payout of that same sector. Two columns, sorted by sector (A to Z).
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.starter: SELECT handle, sector FROM runners ORDER BY sector;
exercises.solution: SELECT handle, sector FROM runners r WHERE payout = (SELECT MAX(payout) FROM runners s WHERE s.sector = r.sector) ORDER BY sector;
exercises.hint: Alias the outer table r and the inner table s; link them with WHERE s.sector = r.sector.
exercises.lore: Every block has one name the drones whisper.

exercises.title: ABOVE THE BLOCK
exercises.prompt: Return the **handle** and **sector** of every runner whose **payout is above the average payout of their OWN sector**. Use a **correlated subquery** (the inner AVG must filter to the outer row's sector). Two columns, sorted by handle (A to Z).
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.starter: SELECT handle, sector FROM runners ORDER BY handle;
exercises.solution: SELECT handle, sector FROM runners r WHERE payout > (SELECT AVG(payout) FROM runners s WHERE s.sector = r.sector) ORDER BY handle;
exercises.hint: Correlate the inner AVG to the outer row: (SELECT AVG(payout) FROM runners s WHERE s.sector = r.sector).
exercises.lore: Out-earn your neighbors and the syndicate takes notice.

exercises.title: REP HOTSPOTS
exercises.prompt: First build a per-sector average rep, then keep only the strong sectors. Using a **derived table** in the FROM clause, return **sector** and its **avg_rep** for every sector whose average rep is **70 or higher**. Sort by avg_rep, highest first. The derived table MUST have an alias.
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, handle TEXT, sector TEXT, rep INTEGER, payout INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
  (1,'Glitch','Shibuya',88,42000),
  (2,'Vex','Shibuya',64,15000),
  (3,'Nyx','Kowloon',91,60000),
  (4,'Riser','Kowloon',55,9000),
  (5,'Cipher','Neon Spine',73,30000),
  (6,'Wraith','Neon Spine',40,5000);
CREATE TABLE contracts (id INTEGER, runner_id INTEGER, target TEXT, bounty INTEGER);
INSERT INTO contracts VALUES
  (101,3,'Arasaka Vault',120000),
  (102,1,'Militech Relay',55000),
  (103,3,'Biotechnica Node',80000),
  (104,5,'Petrochem Grid',25000);
exercises.starter: SELECT sector, AVG(rep) AS avg_rep FROM runners GROUP BY sector;
exercises.solution: SELECT sector, avg_rep FROM (SELECT sector, AVG(rep) AS avg_rep FROM runners GROUP BY sector) t WHERE avg_rep >= 70 ORDER BY avg_rep DESC;
exercises.hint: FROM (SELECT sector, AVG(rep) AS avg_rep FROM runners GROUP BY sector) t, then WHERE avg_rep >= 70.
exercises.lore: Where the reputations run hot, the real work hides.
